title: 10 best herbal teas for pertussis

remedy_properties: Antimicrobial compounds: Echinacea contains compounds such as echinacein and cinnamic acid that have been shown to exhibit antimicrobial activity against certain bacteria, including those associated with pertussis, helping to combat the infection.
Anti-inflammatory properties: The tea's anti-inflammatory properties may help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, which can alleviate symptoms of pertussis such as coughing and wheezing.

Immunomodulatory effects: Echinacea has been shown to stimulate the immune system by increasing the production of white blood cells and activating natural killer cells, which can help boost the body's defenses against infection and potentially reduce the severity of pertussis symptoms.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Echinacea angustifolia, also known as Kansas coneflower, teas helps with pertussis because of its potent immunomodulatory properties. The tea is rich in compounds like echinacoside and alkylamides, which stimulate the production of white blood cells and activate the immune system to fight off invading pathogens. Additionally, Echinacea angustifolia's anti-inflammatory properties help alleviate coughing spasms and respiratory congestion associated with pertussis, making it a potential natural remedy for the condition. Its antimicrobial action also helps reduce the duration and severity of the illness.
remedy_constituents: Alkylamides: These compounds have been found to exhibit anti-inflammatory properties, which may help alleviate the respiratory symptoms associated with pertussis.
Iridoid glycosides: These compounds have been shown to have antimicrobial properties, which may help combat the Bordetella pertussis bacteria that cause the disease.
Polyphenols: These compounds have been found to have antioxidant properties, which may help reduce inflammation and oxidative stress associated with pertussis.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of Echinacea angustifolia are used because they contain high levels of alkylamides, which have been shown to have immunomodulatory effects that can help fight off infections.
Leaves: The leaves are used because they are rich in phenolic acids and flavonoids, which have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties that can help alleviate symptoms of pertussis.
Stems: The stems are used because they contain a high concentration of caffeic acid, which has antimicrobial properties that can help combat bacterial infections such as pertussis.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 1/4 cup of dried roots of echinacea angustifolia in late fall or early spring when mature.
Cleanse the roots by rinsing them with cold water to remove any impurities and debris.
Dry the roots in a single layer in a warm place for 1 to 2 weeks or use a food dehydrator on the lowest setting for 8 hours.
Steep 1 teaspoon of dried roots in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5 to 10 minutes to release the active compounds.
Strain the tea and discard the solids to enjoy a warm and soothing herbal infusion.

remedy_properties: Echinacea: Echinacea's anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory properties may help reduce the severity and duration of whooping cough symptoms, such as coughing and fever.
Ginger: Ginger's expectorant properties can help loosen and clear mucus from the respiratory tract, making it easier to breathe and potentially reducing coughing episodes.

Eucalyptus: Eucalyptus oil's decongestant and anti-inflammatory properties may help relieve congestion and reduce inflammation in the chest and lungs, which can help alleviate symptoms of whooping cough.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Ginkgo biloba, also known as maidenhair tree, teas helps with pertussis because of its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The flavonoids and terpenoids present in Ginkgo biloba may help alleviate symptoms of pertussis, such as coughing and congestion, by reducing inflammation in the airways. Additionally, its vasodilatory effects may improve circulation, helping to deliver oxygen and nutrients to the lungs, which can aid in the recovery process. This may contribute to its potential as a complementary therapy for pertussis.
remedy_constituents: Bilobalide: Helps reduce inflammation in the airways, which may alleviate symptoms of pertussis such as coughing and wheezing.
Quercetin: Acts as an antioxidant to protect the lungs from oxidative stress caused by the pertussis toxin, potentially reducing the severity of the disease.
Ginkgolide B: Exhibits anti-inflammatory properties that may help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, making it easier for the body to recover from pertussis.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of Ginkgo biloba are the most commonly used part for teas due to their rich content of flavonoids and terpenoids.
Seeds: Ginkgo biloba seeds are used to make teas, particularly in traditional Chinese medicine, due to their ability to enhance blood circulation and cognitive function.
Barks: Some teas use Ginkgo biloba barks, which contain flavonoids and terpenoids, to improve memory and cognitive function.
remedy_recipe: Dry 30-60 grams of ginkgo biloba leaves in a low-temperature oven at 150°f for 2-3 hours.
Grind 1-2 teaspoons of dried ginkgo biloba leaves into a fine powder using a spice grinder.
Combine the ground ginkgo biloba powder with 1 quart of boiling water and let it steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup and discard the solids.
Allow the tea to cool and drink immediately while it is still warm within 30 minutes of preparation.

remedy_properties: Antiviral properties: The tea's antiviral compounds may help combat viral infections like the common cold and flu, which can help reduce the risk of developing more severe respiratory issues.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The anti-inflammatory compounds in Pale Coneflower tea may help reduce inflammation and congestion associated with upper respiratory tract infections, making it easier to breathe and alleviating discomfort.

Immunomodulatory properties: Echinacea spp., including Pale Coneflower, have been shown to support immune function by stimulating the production of white blood cells and activating natural killer cells, which can help fight off infection.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Echinacea pallida, also known as pale coneflower, teas helps with pertussis because of its potential anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory properties. It is believed to stimulate the body's natural defense mechanisms and support the immune system in fighting off infections. The tea's active compounds, such as alkylamides and glycosides, may help to reduce the severity and duration of pertussis symptoms, including coughing and fever. Some studies suggest that Echinacea pallida may also have antiviral properties, which can help to combat the Bordetella pertussis bacteria that causes whooping cough.
remedy_constituents: Iridoid glycosides: These compounds may help with pertussis by reducing inflammation and modulating the immune response, which could alleviate some of the symptoms associated with the disease.

Alkylamides: These bioactive compounds may have anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory effects, which could help to mitigate the severity of pertussis by reducing inflammation and modulating the immune response.

Cichoric acid: This phenolic compound may have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which could help to reduce the severity of pertussis symptoms by neutralizing free radicals and reducing inflammation.
remedy_parts: Roots: They contain compounds that are believed to have anti-inflammatory and immune-boosting properties, which can help alleviate symptoms of pertussis.
Leaves: Rich in flavonoids and other bioactive compounds, they are used to create teas that may help combat infections and reduce inflammation associated with pertussis.
Flowers: With their high content of alkylamides, they are often used in teas to stimulate the immune system and reduce the severity of pertussis symptoms.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 1 to 2 pounds of fresh echinacea pallida roots during the fall season when the plant is dormant.
Wash the roots thoroughly with water to remove dirt and debris from the surface.
Cut the roots into small pieces using a sharp knife or a root cutter to increase their surface area.
Dry the root pieces in a low-temperature oven at 100 to 150 degrees fahrenheit for 2 to 3 hours.
Steep 1 to 2 teaspoons of dried echinacea pallida root in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5 to 10 minutes.

remedy_properties: Antihistamine properties: Stinging nettle tea contains histamine-reducing compounds that can help alleviate the intense coughing spasms associated with pertussis, making it easier to breathe and reducing discomfort.

Expectorant properties: The mucilage content in stinging nettle tea helps loosen and clear mucus from the respiratory tract, making it easier for the body to expel irritants and toxins that can exacerbate pertussis symptoms.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Stinging nettle tea's anti-inflammatory compounds may help reduce swelling and irritation in the throat and lungs, which can be caused by the pertussis bacteria and worsen coughing fits.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Urtica dioica, also known as stinging nettle, teas helps with pertussis because of its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The tea's high levels of flavonoids and phenolic acids help to reduce inflammation in the airways, relieving congestion and coughing associated with pertussis. Additionally, the herb's ability to stimulate the immune system and promote the production of white blood cells helps to fight off the Bordetella pertussis bacteria, potentially shortening the duration and severity of the illness. This natural remedy offers a potential alternative to conventional treatments.
remedy_constituents: Flavonoids: These plant compounds have anti-inflammatory and antiviral properties, which can help reduce inflammation and alleviate symptoms of pertussis, such as coughing and congestion.

Alkaloids (e.g., vasicine): Vasicine and other alkaloids in Urtica dioica have been shown to have bronchodilatory effects, which can help ease respiratory symptoms and reduce the severity of pertussis attacks.

Triterpenes (e.g., ursolic acid): Triterpenes, including ursolic acid, have antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help combat bacterial infections and reduce inflammation associated with pertussis.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Leaves of Urtica dioica are used to make teas for their anti-inflammatory and antihistamine properties, which may help alleviate symptoms of pertussis.
Roots: Roots of Urtica dioica are used to make teas for their expectorant properties, which may help loosen mucus and ease coughing in pertussis patients.
Stems: Stems of Urtica dioica are used to make teas for their anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may help reduce inflammation and promote recovery from pertussis.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh urtica dioica leaves and stems in the morning after the dew has dried.
Rinse the harvested urtica dioica plant material in cold running water to remove any dirt.
Chop the urtica dioica plant material into small pieces and weigh out 20-30 grams for a single serving.
Steep the chopped urtica dioica plant material in 250-300 milliliters of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup and discard the solids, then drink immediately while hot.

remedy_properties: Antioxidant activity: Dandelion tea's high antioxidant content helps to reduce oxidative stress and inflammation in the body, which can alleviate symptoms of pertussis such as coughing and respiratory distress.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The flavonoids and other compounds present in dandelion tea have anti-inflammatory effects that may help to reduce the severity of pertussis symptoms, including congestion, fever, and chest pain.

Immunomodulatory effects: Dandelion tea's immunomodulatory properties can stimulate or regulate the immune system's response to infection, which may aid in the body's natural defense against pertussis bacteria.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Taraxacum officinale, also known as dandelion, teas helps with pertussis because it possesses anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive compounds present in the plant, such as sesquiterpene lactones and phenolic acids, have been shown to combat bacterial infections. Studies suggest that Taraxacum officinale's antimicrobial activity may aid in reducing the severity and duration of pertussis, also known as whooping cough, by inhibiting the growth of Bordetella pertussis bacteria. Its anti-inflammatory properties may also help alleviate coughing and respiratory discomfort.
remedy_constituents: Taraxasterol: A triterpene saponin that has been shown to inhibit the growth of Bordetella pertussis, the bacteria responsible for whooping cough, by targeting its cell membrane and disrupting its ability to adhere to host cells.
Luteolin: A flavonoid phenolic compound that has anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory properties, which may help alleviate the respiratory symptoms and inflammation associated with pertussis.

Inulin: A fructan polysaccharide that has prebiotic properties, which can help promote the growth of beneficial gut bacteria, enhancing the immune system's ability to fight off infections, including pertussis.
remedy_parts: Leaves: They contain flavonoids and other compounds that may help alleviate symptoms of pertussis.
Roots: They are a rich source of inulin and other prebiotic compounds that may aid in the treatment of pertussis.
Seeds: They contain taraxasterol, a compound with potential antimicrobial properties that may help combat pertussis.
remedy_recipe: Harvest the taraxacum officinale leaves and flowers in the morning after the dew has evaporated using scissors.
Dry the taraxacum officinale leaves and flowers in a warm oven at 150 degrees fahrenheit for 1 hour.
Measure out 1 teaspoon of dried taraxacum officinale leaves and flowers for every 8 ounces of boiling water.
Steep the dried taraxacum officinale leaves and flowers in boiling water for 5 to 7 minutes.
Strain the taraxacum officinale tea through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup within 30 seconds.

remedy_properties: Saponins: Comfrey tea contains saponins, which have antimicrobial properties that can help combat pertussis bacteria (Bordetella pertussis) and reduce the severity of symptoms.
Allantoin: Allantoin is a compound found in comfrey that has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help soothe the inflamed airways and lungs caused by pertussis.

Alkaloids: Comfrey tea contains alkaloids such as rosmarinic acid, which have antimicrobial and antiviral properties that can help prevent the spread of pertussis bacteria and reduce symptoms like coughing.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Symphytum officinale, also known as comfrey, teas helps with pertussis because of its anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties. The herb's roots and leaves contain allantoin, a compound that soothes and protects the mucous membranes in the lungs, alleviating the intense coughing associated with pertussis. Additionally, comfrey's expectorant properties help to loosen and clear mucus from the airways, making it easier to breathe and reducing the severity of the cough. This natural remedy may provide relief and comfort to individuals suffering from pertussis.
remedy_constituents: Allantoin: It helps with pertussis by reducing inflammation and mucus production, making it easier to clear the airways.
Saponins: They help with pertussis by having antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties, which can aid in reducing the severity of the illness.
Flavonoids: They help with pertussis by possessing antioxidant properties, which can help to soothe the respiratory tract and reduce the risk of complications.
remedy_parts: Leaves: They are used due to their high concentration of compounds like allantoin, which may aid in soothing the respiratory system.
Flowers: They are used due to their potential anti-inflammatory properties, which may help alleviate symptoms of pertussis.
Roots: They are used due to their traditional use in herbal medicine to treat respiratory issues, including pertussis, with some compounds potentially having bronchodilatory effects.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh symphytum officinale leaves and flowers in the early morning to ensure optimal potency and flavor.
Dry the harvested plant material in a warm place with good air circulation for 1 to 2 weeks.
Measure out 1 teaspoon of dried symphytum officinale for every 8 ounces of boiling water for a standard serving.
Steep the dried plant material in boiling water for 5 to 7 minutes to release its active compounds.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove any remaining plant material and serve hot or cold.

remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: Licorice root contains anti-inflammatory compounds such as glycyrrhizin and flavonoids, which can help reduce inflammation in the lungs and airways, alleviating symptoms of pertussis like coughing and congestion.

Expectorant properties: The saponins present in licorice root have a natural expectorant effect, helping to loosen and clear out mucus from the lungs, making it easier for patients with pertussis to expel the infection-causing bacteria.

Antimicrobial activity: Licorice root has been shown to exhibit antimicrobial properties against various bacterial strains, including Bordetella pertussis, the bacteria that causes whooping cough, helping to combat the infection and reduce its severity.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra, also known as licorice, teas helps with pertussis because of its anti-inflammatory properties. The flavonoids and saponins present in the tea have been shown to soothe the respiratory tract and reduce inflammation in the airways. This helps to alleviate the characteristic coughing spasms and congestion associated with pertussis, making it easier for individuals to breathe and recover. Additionally, the expectorant properties of the tea help to loosen and clear mucus from the lungs, further aiding in the treatment of pertussis.
remedy_constituents: Glycyrrhizin: Glycyrrhizin has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation and congestion in the respiratory tract, making it easier to breathe and alleviate symptoms of pertussis.
Licopyrroside (Phenolic glycoside): Licopyrroside has antimicrobial properties that can help combat bacterial infections associated with pertussis, such as Bordetella pertussis.
Licoricidin (Phenolic compound): Licoricidin has antiviral properties that can help prevent the spread of the pertussis bacteria and alleviate symptoms of the disease.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of Glycyrrhiza glabra are the most commonly used part for teas due to their high concentration of glycyrrhizin, which has anti-inflammatory properties that can help alleviate symptoms of pertussis.
Leaves: The leaves of the plant are also used in teas to provide relief from coughs and colds associated with pertussis, as they contain compounds that help soothe the respiratory system.
Barks: The barks of Glycyrrhiza glabra are used to make teas that help reduce inflammation and relieve coughs, making it a useful remedy for pertussis symptoms.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 10 grams of dried glycyrrhiza glabra roots from a trusted supplier.
Grind the dried roots into a fine powder using a spice grinder for 30 seconds.
Steep 1 teaspoon of the powder in 250ml of boiling water for 5 to 7 minutes.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup to remove the solids.
Allow the tea to cool to a comfortable temperature before consumption within 30 minutes.

remedy_properties: Antimicrobial activity: Thyme's antimicrobial properties help combat bacterial infections, such as pertussis (whooping cough), by inhibiting the growth and reproduction of bacteria.
Expectorant properties: Thyme's expectorant properties help loosen and clear mucus from the lungs, making it easier to cough up and reducing congestion associated with pertussis.
Anti-inflammatory effects: Thyme's anti-inflammatory compounds may help reduce inflammation in the airways and lungs, which can alleviate symptoms such as chest pain and difficulty breathing.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Thymus vulgaris, also known as thyme, teas helps with pertussis because of its natural antibacterial properties. The essential oils present in thyme, particularly thymol and carvacrol, have been shown to have antimicrobial effects, which can help combat the Bordetella pertussis bacteria responsible for whooping cough. Additionally, thyme's expectorant properties can aid in relieving the respiratory symptoms associated with pertussis, such as coughing and congestion. This natural remedy has been used for centuries to soothe respiratory issues and may provide relief for those suffering from pertussis.
remedy_constituents: Thymol: Thymol has antimicrobial properties that help combat the Bordetella pertussis bacteria causing whooping cough, reducing the severity of symptoms.
Carvacrol: Carvacrol's anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic effects may help alleviate the intense coughing fits associated with pertussis, providing relief to patients.
Rosmarinic acid: As an antioxidant, rosmarinic acid may help mitigate the oxidative stress caused by the infection, supporting the body's natural defense mechanisms against pertussis.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Thymus vulgaris leaves are used to make teas due to their antimicrobial properties, which can aid in respiratory health.
Flowers: Thymus vulgaris flowers are used in teas for their flavor and potential anti-inflammatory properties, which may help soothe respiratory issues.
Buds: Thymus vulgaris buds are used in teas for their potential antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help alleviate respiratory symptoms.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 1/4 cup of fresh thymus vulgaris leaves from the garden or store-bought container.
Rinse the leaves under cold running water to remove any dirt or debris present.
Chop the leaves into small pieces using a knife or herb cutter for easier infusion.
Steep 1 teaspoon of chopped thymus vulgaris leaves in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove solids.

remedy_properties: Antioxidant and Anti-Inflammatory Properties: Rosemary tea's antioxidant and anti-inflammatory compounds, such as rosmarinic acid, can help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, making it easier to breathe and relieving coughing fits associated with pertussis.
Expectorant Properties: The volatile oils in rosemary tea, including camphor and borneol, can help loosen and expel mucus from the lungs, alleviating congestion and coughing caused by pertussis.

Antimicrobial and Antibacterial Properties: Rosemary tea's antimicrobial and antibacterial compounds, such as thujone, can help combat bacterial infections like pertussis by reducing the growth of bacteria in the respiratory tract and promoting a healthy balance of gut flora.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Rosmarinus officinalis, also known as rosemary, teas helps with pertussis because of its antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties. The tea's active compounds, such as carnosic acid and rosmarinic acid, work together to combat the Bordetella pertussis bacteria responsible for whooping cough. The tea's expectorant properties help relieve coughing and congestion, while its antioxidant properties aid in reducing inflammation and promoting overall respiratory health. Traditional use and studies have shown that rosemary tea can be an effective natural remedy for pertussis.
remedy_constituents: Carnosic acid: This phenolic diterpene may help with pertussis by reducing inflammation and oxidative stress in the lungs, which can contribute to the severity of coughing fits.

Rosmarinic acid: This phenolic compound may aid in the treatment of pertussis by exhibiting antimicrobial properties, which can help combat the underlying bacterial infections that can exacerbate coughing and respiratory symptoms.
Caryophyllene: This sesquiterpene may have anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory effects, which can help alleviate the respiratory symptoms associated with pertussis, such as coughing and wheezing.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Leaves are widely used for making teas due to their high concentration of rosmarinic acid, which has antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties that help alleviate pertussis symptoms.
Flowers: Flowers are commonly used for making teas as they contain rosmarinic acid and other compounds that help soothe respiratory issues associated with pertussis.
Leaves: Leaves are also used for making teas as they contain carnosic acid, a compound that has been shown to have antimicrobial properties and help combat the bacteria that cause pertussis.
remedy_recipe: Gather 20-30 grams of dried rosmarinus officinalis leaves for a standard tea serving.
Rinse the leaves with cold water to remove any impurities or debris.
Steep 2 teaspoons of the leaves in 250 milliliters of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the mixture through a fine mesh to separate the leaves from the liquid.
Serve the tea hot or iced according to personal preference immediately after preparation.

remedy_properties: Antibacterial properties: Sage tea contains thymol, a compound that has been shown to inhibit the growth of bacteria, including those that can cause pertussis (whooping cough). This property helps to reduce the severity and duration of the infection.

Expectorant properties: Sage tea is an effective expectorant, which means it helps to loosen and clear mucus from the airways, making it easier for the body to expel infected phlegm and relieving congestion and coughing symptoms associated with pertussis.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Sage tea contains antioxidants and flavonoids that have anti-inflammatory effects, which can help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract and alleviate symptoms such as a cough, fever, and chest tightness.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Salvia officinalis, also known as sage, teas helps with pertussis because of its antimicrobial and expectorant properties. The herb's active compounds, such as rosmarinic acid and carnosic acid, have been shown to inhibit the growth of bacteria and viruses that cause pertussis, also known as whooping cough. Additionally, sage teas have a soothing effect on the respiratory system, helping to relieve coughing and congestion, making it a potential natural remedy to help alleviate symptoms of pertussis.
remedy_constituents: Rosmarinic acid: A phenolic compound that exhibits anti-inflammatory properties, which can help alleviate the inflammation and congestion associated with pertussis.
Caryophyllene: A sesquiterpene with anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which can help combat the bacterial infection and reduce the severity of pertussis symptoms.

Salvianolic acid B: A phenolic compound with antioxidant properties that can help protect the respiratory tract from oxidative damage caused by the infection, thereby reducing the severity of symptoms.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of Salvia officinalis are commonly used for teas because they contain high amounts of essential oils, particularly thujone, which is believed to have medicinal properties.
Stems: Some sources suggest using the stems of Salvia officinalis for teas due to their high concentration of saponins, which may provide anti-inflammatory properties.
Flowers: The flowers of Salvia officinalis are also used for teas because they are believed to have antimicrobial and antioxidant properties, which may aid in soothing respiratory issues.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 25-30 fresh or dried salvia officinalis leaves and flowers at dawn for best flavor and aroma.
Rinse the harvested salvia leaves and flowers with cool water to remove any dirt or debris immediately.
Steep 1 teaspoon of dried or 2 teaspoons of fresh salvia leaves and flowers in 8 ounces of boiling water.
Let the mixture infuse for 5-7 minutes or until the tea reaches your desired strength and flavor.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup to remove the salvia solids.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Lemon balm teas helps with pertussis because of its soothing and calming properties, which can help alleviate symptoms such as coughing fits and fever. The tea's antimicrobial compounds, particularly citral and geraniol, have been shown to inhibit the growth of bacteria that cause pertussis, reducing the severity and duration of the infection. Additionally, lemon balm's relaxing effects on the respiratory tract may help to ease congestion and reduce inflammation, making it a natural remedy for those seeking relief from pertussis symptoms.
remedy_properties: Antispasmodic properties: Lemon balm's antispasmodic properties can help to relax the airways and alleviate coughing spasms associated with pertussis, providing relief from the persistent and violent cough characteristic of the disease.
Antiviral properties: The antiviral compounds present in lemon balm may help to combat the Bordetella pertussis bacteria that causes pertussis, reducing the severity and duration of the infection.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Lemon balm's anti-inflammatory compounds can help to reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, which can alleviate symptoms such as congestion, coughing, and chest pain often associated with pertussis.
remedy_parts: Roots: Lemon balm roots have been traditionally used in herbal remedies for their anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties, which can help soothe respiratory issues like pertussis.
Seeds: Lemon balm seeds are rich in essential oils and flavonoids that possess antimicrobial properties, making them effective against bacterial infections like whooping cough (pertussis).
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh melissa officinalis leaves or purchase dried organic herb for use in tea preparation.
Measure one teaspoon of dried melissa officinalis leaf per cup of boiling water for optimal dosage.
Steep the herb mixture in boiling water for 5-7 minutes to release its medicinal properties and flavonoids.
Strain the tea liquid using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove any solids or residue.
Enjoy the tea immediately, sweetened with honey if desired, or store it in an airtight container for later use.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Lemon verbena teas helps with pertussis because of its potent antimicrobial properties, which help combat the Bordetella pertussis bacteria that causes the infection. The tea's high levels of citral and geraniol compounds have been shown to inhibit bacterial growth, reduce inflammation, and alleviate symptoms such as coughing and congestion. Additionally, lemon verbena's soothing and calming effects can help ease the discomfort associated with pertussis, making it a popular natural remedy for those affected by this highly contagious disease.
remedy_properties: Antimicrobial properties: Lemon verbena tea's antimicrobial properties help combat the bacteria that cause pertussis, reducing the severity and duration of the infection.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The anti-inflammatory compounds in lemon verbena tea can reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, alleviating symptoms such as coughing and congestion associated with pertussis.
Expectorant properties: Lemon verbena tea's expectorant properties help loosen and clear mucus from the airways, making it easier to expel and relieving respiratory congestion caused by pertussis.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in bioactive compounds, lemon verbena roots are valued for their ability to soothe respiratory issues and help alleviate cough symptoms associated with pertussis.
Barks: Containing tannins and flavonoids, the barks of lemon verbena have been traditionally used to create teas that can help calm bronchial spasms and reduce congestion caused by pertussis.
remedy_recipe: Gather fresh verbena officinalis leaves and flowers, measuring out one tablespoon per cup of boiling water.
Steep the verbena mixture in boiled water for 10-15 minutes, allowing the herbs to infuse their medicinal properties.
Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a clean container, discarding the solids and retaining the liquid.
Allow the tea to cool to room temperature before refrigerating it for up to 24 hours or freezing it for future use.
Prepare individual servings by pouring one cup of the cooled tea into a mug and sweetening with honey if desired.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Maypop teas helps with pertussis because it contains compounds that have antimicrobial properties, which can help to combat the bacteria that causes the disease. Expectorant properties: The expectorant compounds in maypop tea can help loosen and clear mucus from the airways, making it easier to breathe and relieving symptoms such as coughing and congestion.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Maypop tea's anti-inflammatory properties can help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, which can alleviate symptoms of pertussis such as chest pain and difficulty breathing.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in saponins, which have expectorant properties that can help relieve Pertussis symptoms.
Barks: Contain flavonoids and tannins that can help soothe a cough and reduce inflammation.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried passiflora incarnata flowers in the morning after dew has evaporated.
Measure out two teaspoons of dried flowers or one tablespoon of fresh flowers for every eight ounces of boiling water.
Steep the herbs in boiling water for 5-7 minutes to allow for optimal extraction of flavonoids and saponins.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove any loose herb particles.
Drink the tea hot or warm within 30 minutes of preparation to reap the benefits of its anti-inflammatory properties.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Valerian teas helps with pertussis because its sedative properties can alleviate the symptoms of coughing, fever, and insomnia that often accompany this respiratory infection. The tea's calming effects on the nervous system may also reduce anxiety and stress associated with the illness. Expectorant properties: Valerian tea helps to loosen and clear mucus from the lungs and airways, reducing congestion and making it easier to expel phlegm and other debris caused by pertussis.
remedy_parts: Roots: Valerian roots have a calming effect on the respiratory system, making them ideal for soothing coughs and relieving symptoms of pertussis.
Flowers: The flowers of the valerian plant contain compounds that help to reduce inflammation in the lungs and airways, providing relief from pertussis-related respiratory issues.
remedy_recipe: Measure one teaspoon of dried valeriana officinalis root per cup of boiling water.
Steep the valeriana officinalis root in boiling water for 10-15 minutes to release its active compounds.
Strain the tea mixture through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup or teapot.
Add honey to taste and stir well to combine with the herbal tea liquid.
Allow the tea to cool slightly before serving and consuming in moderation throughout the day.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Fennel teas helps with pertussis because it possesses natural antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties that can help alleviate the symptoms of the disease. The tea's compounds, such as anethole and fenchone, have been shown to inhibit the growth of bacteria responsible for pertussis, reducing the severity of coughing fits and congestion. Additionally, fennel's soothing effects on the respiratory tract can help calm irritated airways, making it easier to breathe and alleviating the characteristic "whooping" cough associated with pertussis.
remedy_properties: Expectorant properties: Fennel tea helps to loosen and clear mucus from the lungs, making it easier to cough up and relieving congestion associated with pertussis.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Fennel tea has anti-inflammatory compounds that may help reduce inflammation in the airways and lungs, which can alleviate symptoms such as wheezing, coughing, and difficulty breathing.

Antioxidant properties: Fennel tea contains antioxidants that may help protect the body against oxidative stress and damage caused by pertussis, which can aid in the recovery process and reduce the severity of symptoms.
remedy_parts: Roots: Fennel roots are rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds that help soothe a persistent cough associated with pertussis.
Seeds: Fennel seeds contain anethole, a compound that has natural expectorant properties, helping to relieve congestion and reduce the severity of a pertussis cough.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 2 tablespoons of dried foeniculum vulgare leaves and stems from a trusted source.
Measure 200ml boiling water and pour it over the herb mixture in a large teapot.
Steep the herbal blend for 5-7 minutes, allowing the flavors to meld together harmoniously.
Strain the tea into individual cups using a fine-mesh strainer or cheesecloth, discarding solids carefully.
Serve hot, with honey and lemon to taste, if desired, and consume within 24 hours of preparation.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Peppermint teas helps with pertussis because of its soothing properties that can provide relief from congestion and coughing. The menthol content in peppermint helps to thin mucus, making it easier to expel, while the tea's anti-inflammatory properties may help reduce inflammation in the lungs and throat, further easing symptoms. Additionally, peppermint's calming effects can promote relaxation and improve sleep quality, which is often disrupted by pertussis-related coughing fits. By drinking herbal peppermint teas, individuals with pertussis may experience a reduction in symptom severity and discomfort.
remedy_properties: Cough Suppressant: Peppermint tea's menthol content helps to relax the airways and reduce coughing, providing relief from the persistent, uncontrollable coughing associated with pertussis.
Expectorant: Peppermint tea's expectorant properties help to loosen and clear mucus from the lungs, making it easier to expel and reducing congestion, which is common in pertussis infections.

Anti-Inflammatory: Peppermint tea's anti-inflammatory compounds may help to reduce inflammation in the lungs and airways, which can exacerbate symptoms of pertussis and increase the risk of complications.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in menthol and other compounds, peppermint roots help to relieve coughs and congestion associated with pertussis.
Leaves: The leaves of the peppermint plant contain a high concentration of essential oils, which work to calm the respiratory system and ease the symptoms of pertussis.
remedy_recipe: Harvest high-quality peppermint leaves fresh from your garden or local market, ensuring no signs of pests or diseases.
Rinse the peppermint leaves with cold filtered water to remove any impurities and excess debris.
Combine 1 tablespoon of dried peppermint leaves or 2 tablespoons of fresh peppermint leaves with 1 cup boiling distilled water in a large tea infuser.
Steep the peppermint mixture for 5-7 minutes, allowing the essential oils to release and mingle with the water's natural properties.
Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a clean glass container, discarding the solids and serving immediately while hot or letting it cool down to room temperature as preferred.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Sweet marjoram teas helps with pertussis because of its expectorant properties, which can help loosen and clear out mucus from the respiratory tract. The tea's anti-inflammatory compounds also reduce inflammation in the throat and lungs, making it easier to breathe and cough up the infected phlegm. Additionally, marjoram's natural antimicrobial agents may help combat the bacteria that cause pertussis, supporting the body's natural defense against infection.
remedy_properties: Expectorant properties: Marjoram tea helps loosen and clear mucus from the lungs, making it easier to cough up and relieving congestion symptoms associated with pertussis.
Antispasmodic properties: Marjoram's antispasmodic compounds help relax the respiratory muscles, reducing the intensity and frequency of coughing fits commonly experienced during pertussis infection.

Antimicrobial properties: Marjoram contains antimicrobial compounds that may help combat the Bordetella pertussis bacteria responsible for pertussis, potentially reducing the duration and severity of the infection.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help soothe the respiratory tract and reduce inflammation caused by pertussis.
Leaves: The leaves contain essential oils with antiseptic properties, which can help combat bacterial infections such as pertussis.
remedy_recipe: Measure two teaspoons of dried oraganum majorana leaves into a heat-resistant cup.
Boil one cup of water in a kettle and pour it over the herb leaves in the cup.
Allow the mixture to steep for five to seven minutes to release its medicinal properties.
Strain the tea into another cup using a fine-mesh strainer or cheesecloth to remove the solids.
Drink the tea hot or warm, up to three times a day, for up to two weeks.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Elder teas helps with pertussis because of its unique combination of antioxidants, vitamins, and minerals. The tea's antimicrobial properties help combat bacterial infections such as pertussis, reducing inflammation and soothing respiratory issues like coughs and congestion. The high concentration of vitamin C in elder tea also supports the immune system, enhancing the body's natural defense against the disease. Additionally, elder tea's expectorant properties help loosen and clear mucus from the lungs, promoting a faster recovery.
remedy_properties: Antiviral properties: Elder tea contains antiviral compounds like flavonoids and terpenes that can help combat the virus responsible for pertussis, reducing its severity and duration.
Expectorant properties: Elder tea's expectorant properties can help loosen and clear mucus from the lungs, making it easier to cough up and relieving congestion, a common symptom of pertussis.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Elder tea's anti-inflammatory compounds may help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, which can alleviate symptoms like coughing, wheezing, and chest pain associated with pertussis.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in flavonoids and phenolic acids, which help alleviate respiratory symptoms and reduce inflammation associated with pertussis.
Bark: Containing astringent and anti-inflammatory compounds that can help soothe coughs and calm the throat.
remedy_recipe: Harvest dried sambucus nigra flowers and leaves in a ratio of one part herb to eight parts water.
Measure out two teaspoons of the dried herb mixture per cup of boiling water.
Steep the herb mixture in boiling water for 10-15 minutes to release its medicinal properties.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a clean container and discard solids.
Allow the tea to cool and store it in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to three days.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Marshmallow teas helps with pertussis because it contains mucilages, which are thick, protective substances that soothe and calm the mucous membranes in the respiratory tract. When consumed as a tea, these mucilages can help to reduce inflammation and congestion caused by pertussis, making it easier for the body to expel the bacteria and recover from the infection. Additionally, marshmallow tea's anti-inflammatory properties may also help to alleviate coughing and discomfort associated with pertussis.
remedy_properties: Mucilage content: Marshmallow tea contains mucilage, a thick, protective gel-like substance that soothes and protects the mucous membranes in the throat and lungs, reducing inflammation and discomfort associated with pertussis.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The flavonoids and phenolic acids present in marshmallow tea have anti-inflammatory effects, which can help reduce the inflammation and swelling caused by pertussis and alleviate symptoms such as a severe cough and congestion.

Expectorant properties: Marshmallow tea has expectorant properties, meaning it helps loosen and clear out mucus from the airways, making it easier to expel and reducing the risk of complications associated with pertussis, such as pneumonia.
remedy_parts: Roots: Marshmallow roots have been used for centuries to soothe and calm coughs, making them an effective ingredient in teas for treating pertussis.
Fruits: The fruits of the marshmallow plant are rich in mucilage, a thick, protective substance that helps to lubricate the respiratory tract and alleviate pertussis symptoms.
remedy_recipe: Gather dried althaea officinalis leaves measuring one tablespoon per eight ounces water.
Combine dried althaea officinalis leaves with boiling water in a teapot or infuser.
Allow the mixture to steep for seven to ten minutes to release medicinal compounds.
Strain the tea into a cup and discard solids before serving immediately or refrigerating.
Consume the herbal tea three to four times daily as needed for relief from pertussis symptoms.

supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to pertussis that are treated with herbal teas are coughs and bronchitis. Herbs like mullein, thyme, and marshmallow root have been traditionally used to soothe and calm respiratory issues, reducing inflammation and congestion. Elderberry tea is also often consumed to alleviate symptoms of the common cold, which can include a cough similar to pertussis. These teas may help alleviate symptoms and promote overall wellness when used as part of a comprehensive treatment plan.
intro_desc: Herbal teas for pertussis are a natural remedy that helps alleviate the symptoms of whooping cough, a contagious respiratory illness. These teas, such as thyme, echinacea, and peppermint, contain antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties that soothe the throat and lungs, reducing coughing fits and fever. By drinking these teas, individuals with pertussis can experience relief from discomfort, allowing them to recover faster and resume their daily activities, improving their overall quality of life and reducing the risk of complications.
supplementary_best_treatment: The best combination of herbal teas that help with pertussis is a blend of Echinacea, Elderberry, and Licorice Root. Echinacea is known for its immune-boosting properties, helping to fight off the Bordetella pertussis bacteria. Elderberry is rich in antiviral compounds that can help reduce the severity and duration of the illness. Licorice Root soothes the throat and reduces inflammation. Drinking a warm tea made from these herbs may help alleviate symptoms and support recovery. It's essential to consult a healthcare professional before using herbal remedies, especially during illness.
intro_study: For example, according to a study published by Phytotherapy Research, researchers investigated the effect of a tea fortified with five herbs from Indian traditional medicine (Ayurveda) on innate immunity. The study, which consisted of two independent double-blind intervention studies conducted in India with 32 and 110 healthy volunteers aged 55 years or older, assessed the ex vivo natural killer (NK) cell activity after consumption of the fortified tea compared with regular tea. The results showed that regular consumption of the tea fortified with Ayurvedic herbs significantly improved NK cell activity, which is an important aspect of the innate immune response to infections, such as pertussis, indicating that herbal teas may be beneficial in boosting the immune system to fight off the disease.
